coredata.dc:title: Icosahedral transition metal clusters (M13, M = Fe, Ni, and Cu) adsorbed on graphene quantum dots, a DFT study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Montejo-Alvaro, F.
Oliva, J.
Zarate, A.
Herrera-Trejo, M.
Hdz-García, H.M.
Mtz-Enriquez, A.I.
coredata.dc:description: We report a first-principles investigation of metal clusters (Ni13, Cu13 and Fe13) adsorbed on pristine and defective graphene quantum dots (QDs). It was observed that the presence of one vacancy in the QD increased the binding energy of the metal cluster as well as the electronic conductivity of the graphene. Among the metal clusters, the Cu13 cluster produced the highest electrical conductivity on graphene and the lowest values of binding energies, which in turn, favored the chemical reactivity of the graphene QDs. Furthermore, the types of bonds produced between the metal clusters and the pristine or defective graphene were analyzed with the electron localized function (ELF) and non-covalent interaction (NCI) plots. The calculations presented here give an insight about the electronic and chemical properties of graphene interacting with metal clusters which are of interest for catalytic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal clusters
Graphene
Benzene
Coronene

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction of graphene QDs and the M13 clusters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized geometries and spin multiplicity for the metal clusters.
Optimized geometries for the interaction of: benzene with a) Fe13, b) Ni13, and c) Cu13, and coronene with d) Fe13, e) Ni13, and f) Cu13.

(a) The three different sites where the carbon vacancy can be allocated in the coronene molecule (see arrows). The optimized geometries for the interaction of the coronene molecule with a C vacancy (site 1) with the (a) Fe13, (b) Ni13 and (c) Cu13 clusters.
ELF contours for: a) Fe13, b) Ni13, c) Cu13, d) Fe13/C6H6, e) Ni13/C6H6, f) Cu13/C6H6, g) Fe13/C24H6, h) Ni13/C24H12, i) Cu13/C24H12, j) Fe13/C23H12, k) Ni13/C23H12, and l) Cu13/C24H12 clusters.
NCI plots showing the interaction among the benzene and a) Fe13, b) Ni13, and c) Cu13; coronene and d) Fe13, e) Ni13, and f) Cu13; and defective coronene and g) Fe13, h) Ni13, and i) Cu13.
Gupta parameters for the Fe13, Ni13 and Cu13 clusters.

Values of total QTAIM charge and HOMO-LUMO band gap calculated in the most stable configuration using the functional BLYP and the basis set SV(P). QTAIM is the total charge transferred from cluster. The positive values indicate a charge transfer from the cluster to the CxHy molecules. M denotes the multiplicity of the complexes and <d> the average bond lengths among the C atoms of the QDs and the closer metal atoms form the M13 clusters; the number of bonds among the TM and C atoms in the complexes are in brackets. G and dG stand for DFT calculations for graphene and monovacancy defective graphene superlattices, respectively.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Experimental data for Fe+-ligand organometallic complexes.

head.ce:title: Icosahedral transition metal clusters (M13, M = Fe, Ni, and Cu) adsorbed on graphene quantum dots, a DFT study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cinvestav Unidad Saltillo, Av. Industria Metalúrgica 1062, Parque Industrial, Ramos Arizpe, Coahuila, 25900, Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Física, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad Católica del Norte, Casilla, 1280, Antofagasta, Chile

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Corporación Mexicana de Investigación en Materiales S.A. de C.V.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a first-principles investigation of metal clusters (Ni13, Cu13 and Fe13) adsorbed on pristine and defective graphene quantum dots (QDs). It was observed that the presence of one vacancy in the QD increased the binding energy of the metal cluster as well as the electronic conductivity of the graphene. Among the metal clusters, the Cu13 cluster produced the highest electrical conductivity on graphene and the lowest values of binding energies, which in turn, favored the chemical reactivity of the graphene QDs. Furthermore, the types of bonds produced between the metal clusters and the pristine or defective graphene were analyzed with the electron localized function (ELF) and non-covalent interaction (NCI) plots. The calculations presented here give an insight about the electronic and chemical properties of graphene interacting with metal clusters which are of interest for catalytic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a 2D material widely studied for applications in the electronics, medical and environmental fields due to its high mechanical, thermal and electrical properties [1]. Particularly, the graphene films have been useful for the development of new transparent conductive substrates, which can be useful for the fabrication of light emitting devices, solar cells, and thin film transistors [1]. Also, graphene composites decorated with transition metal nanoparticles of Ni, Pt, Fe, and Cu have been used as catalyst for the hydrogen generation or degradation of dangerous dyes [2,3]. Other reports have employed Au and Pt nanoparticles (NPs) to synthesize graphene based nanocomposites for hydrogen evolution or dye degradation by photocatalytic process under visible light but the main inconvenience is the high cost of the Au and Pt NPs [4,5]. The conduction electrons in these metal NPs are able to harvest visible light energy through the effect of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR).

The LSPR allows to enhance not only the light absorption but also produce “hot electrons” with high energy at the surface of metallic NPs, which facilitates the chemical reactions and improve any chemical conversion at ambient conditions [6]. In fact, transition metal NPs with sizes below 100 nm have a higher surface to volume ratio in comparison with their bulk counterparts, in consequence, there are more active sites for catalytic activity [7]. Moreover, the size of these NPs is easy to select with the current synthesis methods and they are redispersible and reusable, which met the actual requirements of modern green catalysis [7]. Although numerous experimental works have reported the use of metal NPs for catalytic or photocatalytic applications, there are only few experimental reports about the understanding of the electronic properties of metal clusters or metal NPs/graphene systems [8,9]. For this reason, several theoretical studies have appeared to understand the electronic properties of single metal clusters or the interaction between the metal NPs and graphene: Piotrowski et al. reported a density functional theory (DFT) study about the atomic structure, binding energies, effective coordination numbers, average bond lengths, and magnetic properties of the 3d, 4d, and 5d metal (30 elements) clusters containing 13 atoms [10]. For this study, the authors employed icosahedral-, hexagonal bilayer- and double simple cubic-like forms and demonstrated new structures of minimum energy; for example, the configuration of minimum energy for the Ni13 and Cu13 clusters was icosahedral. Additionally, their calculations indicated that the 3d systems have more preference for close-packed structures than the 4d and 5d systems [10].

Regarding the systems of metal clusters adsorbed on graphene, Johll et al. reported DFT calculations of Fe, Co, and Ni adatoms and dimers adsorbed on graphene [11]. They calculated the optimum adsorption sites, the structures of minimum energy, the band structures, electronic density of states, as well as the magnetic moments of the adatoms and dimers. One of the most important results was the fact that the binding energies between the dimmers and graphene depended on three factors, mainly: the energy change in the dimer atom farther from graphene after desorption, the charge transfer coefficient and the adsorption site [11]. Further, Sahoo et al. carried out theoretical studies about the structure, stability, and magnetic properties of icosahedral transition metal clusters (TMCs) formed by 13 atoms (TM13, where TM = Fe, Co, Ni), which were deposited on pristine (defect free) and defective graphene sheets with 1 C vacancy [12]. They found: 1) the clusters are more strongly bound to the graphene sheets with defects, which means that the defects are necessary to stabilize the clusters, 2) the binding to the defected graphene sheets did not decrease the cluster magnetic moment significantly compared to that obtained for pristine graphene sheets, and 3) among all the TM13 clusters, Co13 was absorbed better on pristine and defective graphene as compared to the Fe13 and Ni13 clusters [12]. Furthermore, the adsorption of Fe13 and Al13 clusters on defective graphene was studied by Lim et al., they found adsorption energies of −6.98 eV and −3.84 eV for the Fe13 and Al13 clusters on defective graphene, respectively [13]. The authors also concluded that the defects increased the binding energy between the TMCs and the graphene, which means that the defects are necessary to stabilize the clusters [13].

Other interesting theoretical works have proposed the use of metal cluster/graphene systems for applications in catalysis: Wang et al. reported DFT studies of several platinum based alloy nanoparticles (MPt12 NPs) deposited on N-doped defective graphene and found that the MPt12 NPs presented a lower magnetic moment in comparison with the free standing NPs, this in turn, enhances the stability of the deposited MPt12 NPs and promotes the catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction [14]. Lim et al. published theoretical evidence to improve the electrochemical conversion of CO2 to hydrocarbon fuels by using defective graphene decorated with Cu nanoparticles [15]. For this, the graphene support enhanced the electron conduction and avoided the agglomeration of Cu NPs. In addition, Song et al. studied by DFT calculations the catalytic oxidation of CO on Cu embedded on graphene and found that the high activity of the Cu NPs is attributed to the electronic resonance among electronic states of CO, O2, and the Cu atom, particularly among Cu-3d, CO-2π*, and O2-2π* orbitals [16]. Also, Zhou et al. achieved a DFT investigation on CO oxidation by using Au8 or Pt4 clusters on defective graphene, those authors demonstrated that the presence of defects reduced the reaction barrier from 3.0 eV to 0.2 eV, which facilitated the CO oxidation [17]. Moreover, Li et al. investigated the catalytic capabilities of a Fe atom on graphene oxide (GO) and demonstrated that the Fe atoms bond strongly to the oxygen sites. This favor the adsorption of the O2 molecules on Fe/GO, this in turn, favors the catalytic oxidation of CO [18]. Finally, it is worthy to mention that DFT studies about M-N4 clusters (M = Fe, Co and Ni) adsorbed on graphene have demonstrated that such systems are promising for the capture of air pollutants such as ammonia (NH3) or to favor oxygen reduction reactions [19,20].

Currently, the research groups are focusing efforts to find new graphene based photocatalyst with narrow band gap for an increased light absorption [21]. The photocatalytic performance of such composites could be enhanced if they suppress efficiently the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. For this, the presence graphene as a support is a key issue because it has a high surface area, which increases the photocatalytic sites and quickly capture the photogenerated electrons and avoid the charge recombination [22]. However, the weak interaction between the graphene and metal clusters limits the reactivity of the clusters [23]. Therefore, studies about how to tune the electronic interaction in such a way that we can predict the catalytic properties of metal NPs/graphene systems are necessary. In fact, weak adsorption of metal clusters on graphene is not desirable because they can form bigger clusters, which is detrimental for the catalytic processes [23]. For this reason, it is important to find novel systems where the small metal clusters are stable on graphene. Due to this need, our investigation focuses on DFT calculations to unravel the electronic properties of Ni13, Cu13 and Fe13 clusters adsorbed on graphene quantum dots (QDs). The Cu, Ni and Fe elements were chosen because they are low-cost and abundant metals and can be easily synthesized. In fact, the Cu, Ni and Fe metals are very sensitive to oxygen, and when they are combined with graphene, present high photocatalytic reduction for CO2, and can be useful for the synthesis of dimethyl carbonate and for hydrogen generation [24,25]. The present work provides an insight about how the reactivity, electrical conductivity and stability of the metal clusters changed on graphene, which in turn, opens the possibility for the synthesis of new carbon-based catalysts for hydrogen generation or for dye removal, while having high catalytic activity and low cost.

The initial structure of Ni13, Cu13, and Fe13 clusters was determined using the Gupta potentials and was optimized by DFT calculations in the software Orca 3.0.1 [26]. The Gupta parameters A, p, q and r
0 employed for the calculations for each cluster are shown in Table 1 . The coronene (C24H12) structure was considered as model for graphene QDs, and as a smallest unit of graphene, the benzene molecule (C6H6) was also studied. A carbon vacancy was introduced in the coronene structure, giving rise to the neutral C23H12 molecule. All the DFT calculations were done using the Becke-Lee-Yang-Parr (BLYP) functional and SV(P) basis sets [27,28]. The binding energy (E
b ) between the metal cluster and the graphene QDs was calculated with the equation:
(1)
E
b
=
E
C l u s t e r / G r a p h e n e
−
(
E
C l u s t e r
+
E
G r a p h e n e
)
where
E
C l u s t e r / G r a p h e n e
is the energy of the cluster/graphene QDs complexes.
E
C l u s t e r
and
E
G r a p h e n e
are the energies for the cluster and the graphene quantum dot, respectively.

Fig. 1 shows the structures of minimum energy and the spin multiplicity for the M13 clusters. The ground states of the M13 clusters exhibit an icosahedral configuration and presents a Jahn–Teller type distortions, which is in agreement with previous reports [32,33]. The spin multiplicity of the structures with minimum energy was 45, 9, and 6 for Fe13, Ni13, and Cu13 clusters, respectively. These spin multiplicities agree with the reported ground states for the Fe13 [34], Ni13 [35], and Cu13 [35] clusters, respectively. The average distance between the inner and outer atoms was 2.41, 2.32 and 2.42 Å for Fe13, Ni13 and Cu13 clusters, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of graphene QDs and the M13 clusters
ce:section.ce:para: The icosahedral M13 clusters can be adsorbed on the graphene QDs with three possible orientations: on the edges, on the vertex, and on the triangular faces of the icosahedrons. The structures of minimum energy for the M13 clusters on graphene QDs indicate that their interaction is strongest through the triangular faces, except for the Ni13 on graphene when a carbon vacancy is present. The most stable configurations for the interaction between the M13 clusters and the pristine graphene QDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The Ni13 and Cu13 clusters are similarly bonded to benzene, that is, through their triangular face. Here, each atom of the triangular face is bonded with two C atoms (see Fig. 2b and c). In contrast, for the Fe13 cluster, one atom of the triangular face is bonded with only one C atom, see Fig. 2a. When the Cu13 cluster is adsorbed on the coronene molecule, each Cu atom is bonded to only one C atom at the edge of the QD, see Fig. 2f. While the metal atoms of the triangular faces of the Fe13 and Ni13 clusters form bonds with two carbon atoms at the center of the coronene molecule, see Fig. 2d and e.

The C vacancy in the coronene molecule can be introduced in three different positions, see Fig. 3 a. However, the single-point calculations revealed that the vacancy at the positions (2) and (3) are 0.34 and 0.68 eV above than the vacancy at site (1), respectively. For this reason, the coronene with a vacancy at (1) was used as a starting point for the interaction with the M13 clusters. The most stable configurations for the interaction between the M13 clusters and the coronene molecule with a vacancy are shown in Fig. 3. Larger deformations of the graphene QD were caused when the Fe13 and Ni13 clusters interact with coronene with a carbon vacancy, see Fig. 3b and c. The interactions produce a concave geometry for Fe13 (the QD bends towards the cluster) and a convex geometry for Ni13 and Cu13 (the QD bends away from the cluster). The variable bending of the defective graphene QDs has also been reported for a series of M13 clusters, where the C45H15 QDs were concave for the adsorption of Fe13 and Co13, and the Ni13 cluster produces a convex geometry [12]. As seen in Fig. 3d, lower deformation of the convex geometry was found for the adsorption of Cu13, which can be related with larger CCu bond than both CFe and CNi bonds. As shown in Table 2 , the bond lengths and the number of bonds among C and the TM in the complexes are of the same order than those reported by different authors. Additionally, the trend in the bond lengths is similar to that reported for benzene-TM complexes, where larger distances were reported for Cu-benzene complexes [36]; in a similar way, larger distances were related with smaller metal to QDs donation [36].

The values of E
b corresponding to the M13 clusters on graphene QDs were calculated for different spin multiplicities, the binding energies for the ground states are listed in Table 2. For some systems quasi-degenerated states were found, whose difference in energy are below 0.03 eV. For the Fe13 clusters adsorbed on C6H6 and C23H12, quasi-degenerated states were found for spin multiplicities of 37 and 39. The spin multiplicity of the complexes decreases from 45 for the bare Fe13 cluster to 37 for the adsorbed cluster, the decrease in the magnetic moment has been associated to the charge transfer from the cluster to graphene [13]. The E
b for the Fe13/C6H6 and Fe13/C24H12 systems are comparable to those reported for the experimental values for both the Fe+/C6H6 and Fe+/C24H12 complexes [37], see Table 2. For the Fe13/benzene complex, the
E
b
is also comparable to that reported for the Fe7/C6H6 complex [38]. The E
b for the Fe13/C24H12 complex can be compared to that calculated for the Fe13/G complex, where G is a supercell of graphene calculated by PBE [13], see Table 2. On the other hand, the calculated E
b for the Fe13/C23H12 system is higher due to the presence of a vacancy in the graphene QDs, which is very reactive and promotes a strong adsorption of the metal clusters [13]. The E
b for the Fe13/C23H12 system is lower than those obtained in graphene supercells [12,13], but 1.63 eV larger than the reported for the adsorption of Fe13 on C63H21 [12].

The Ni13 clusters adsorbed on the graphene QDs present higher binding energies than those calculated for Fe and Cu clusters, without any quasi-degenerated state, see Table 2. The calculated E
b are slightly higher than those reported for a graphene supercell with two C vacancies [41]. As a sake of comparison, Table 2 shows the experimental E
b value for the adsorption of benzene on Ni (100), which value is more than 2.6 eV lower than for the Ni13/C6. For the case of Cu13, the highest binding energies were found for different spin multiplicities, with two energetically quasi-degenerated states for the Cu13/C24H12 complex, see Table 2. It is worthy to notice that the Cu13 clusters had lower values of binding energy in comparison with the Ni13 and Fe13 clusters. The theoretical E
b for both the Cu13/C24H12 and Cu13/C23H12 systems are close to other theoretical values obtained with the PBE/vdW for supercells of both graphene and monovacancy graphene [40], respectively, see Table 2. Moreover, the HOMO-LUMO (H-L) gap is related to the kinetic stability, the electrical conductivity and the chemical reactivity. Low values of H-L gap suggest a high electrical conductivity, high chemical reactivity, and low stability. The H-L gaps were 5.07 and 2.81 eV for the C6H6 and C24H12, respectively. It indicates that the H-L gap decreases with the size of the molecule. The decrease of the H-L gap value with the increase of the graphene quantum dot size is due to a diminution of the quantum confinement effect. Das et al. reported that the quantum confinement effect decreases for larger diamond shaped graphene QDs [42]. Furthermore, the carbon vacancy drastically reduces the band gap value from 2.81 to 0.70 eV, this means that such defect in the graphene QDs favors its electrical conductivity. When the metal clusters Fe13, Ni13 and Cu13 are deposited on the graphene QDs, all the H-L gaps values decreased as compared with those for the graphene QDs. This result suggests that the presence of metal clusters increases the electrical conductivity of the graphene QDs. Also, a comparison between the different metal/graphene systems suggests that the lowest band gap values are obtained after the adsorption of Cu. Thus, the highest electrical conductivity is produced after the adsorption of Cu, even though these Cu/graphene systems have the lowest binding energy values.

The systems with the lowest electrical conductivity are the Ni13/C6H6, Fe13/C23H12, and the Ni13/C23H12 systems. The trapping of photogenerated electrons and their fast transport are suitable to avoid the charge recombination in photocatalytic applications related with the degradation of dyes or hydrogen generation. Particularly, the graphene decorated with metal NPs is widely employed because the metal nanoparticles promote the light trapping by scattering effects and also can act as charge trapping centers [43]. Additionally, the graphene QDs contribute to the fast charge transport of photogenerated electrons, reducing the charge recombination and enhance the photocatalytic activity [44]. Considering this last statement, our theoretical calculations indicate that for photocatalytic applications, Cu13 nanoparticles are desirable to decorate the graphene QDs, since these NPs enhance the electrical conductivity of graphene. In fact, a previous report indicate that the Cu NPs/graphene composite degrades three times faster the rhodamine dye molecule than other graphene composites such as TiO2/graphene, Fe3O4/graphene, and Bi2WO6/graphene [9].

The Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) has been employed to analyze the intermolecular interactions of the clusters with the graphene QDs using the Multiwfn package [45]. Table 2 shows the values of total charge produced by the interaction among the metal clusters and the graphene QDs. The total charge of the cluster after their adsorption on graphene is positive; it indicates that the electron charge was transferred from the clusters to the graphene QDs. For example, the Ni13 cluster transferred 1.42 e − toward the C atoms in the graphene QDs for the Ni13/C23H12 system. The highest values of the QTAIM charge appear in the systems where the M13 clusters are adsorbed on graphene QDs with a carbon vacancy. The electron charge transferred to the graphene QDs is directly related to the binding energy; i.e., low values of transferred charge implies small binding energies, see Table 2. As was explained above, the charge transference was inferred from the diminishing of magnetic moments from bare clusters to the adsorbed clusters.

The type of bonds among the metal cluster and the graphene QDs were studied using the electronic localization function (ELF), which is useful to find an electronic pair in certain region of the space. The ELF has values from 0 to 1, which indicates the probability to find an electron pair from 0 to 100%. Fig. 4 depicts the ELF plots for all the cluster/graphene systems. The value of ∼0.4 for the Fe13 cluster indicates a high electronic delocalization in the entire cluster (see Fig. 4a). A value of ∼0.3 was found for the Ni13 and Cu13 clusters, suggesting that the electrons around the atoms are slightly delocalized (Fig. 4b and c). Fig. 4d-l illustrate the ELF contours for the M13 clusters on the graphene QDs. The red region between the C atoms in benzene and coronene indicates a covalent CC bond. In general, the yellow to red regions located in the upper part of the two C atoms indicate that the electron pair is closer to the graphene QDs, which are originated by the transference of charge from the clusters, see Fig. 4d-l. For the Fe13 and Ni13 clusters adsorbed on benzene and coronene, and for all the Cu13 complexes, the ELF value is between 0.7 and 0.8, which suggests lower charge transfer from the cluster to the graphene QDs, see Fig. 4d–i and l. On the other hand, for the iron and nickel clusters adsorbed on defective graphene QDs, the ELF values are higher than 0.9 in the upper part of the C atoms, see Fig. 4j and k. The high ELF values near to the C atoms suggest a strong charge transfer from the Ni13 and Fe13 cluster toward the defective graphene, in accordance with the QTAIM charges.

The interaction of the metal clusters and the graphene QDs were analyzed by using the non-covalent interactions (NCI). This analysis is suitable to study the type of interaction produced during the adsorption of the metal clusters. Fig. 5 shows the NCI contours for the M13 clusters adsorbed on the graphene QDs. The Cu13/C24H12 system reveals that the interaction between the Cu13 and coronene is dominated by van der Waals interactions (see green region in Fig. 5f), in accordance with its low adsorption energy −0.62 eV and the low charge transfer of +0.15, see Table 2. However, in the other systems with a high value of E
b , the NCI contours show only repulsive interactions due to steric effect, see the red regions in Fig. 5a–e and 5g-i. This repulsive force appears when two atoms are very close and the orbitals overlap, this in turn, creates the repulsive force [46].

According to the calculations for the structures of minimum energy, the metal clusters (Fe13, Ni13 and Cu13) are interacting with the graphene QDs through their triangular faces. The atoms of the triangular face in the Ni13 and Cu13 clusters are bonded with two C atoms in benzene, while the atoms of the Fe13 cluster is bonded with only 1 C atom. The presence of a carbon vacancy in the coronene molecule increased the adsorption energy of the metal cluster. In general, the highest values of binding energy were observed for the Ni13 clusters while the lowest values were observed for the Cu13 clusters, suggesting that the Cu13/graphene systems are the most reactive. The bonding analysis indicated that the charge transfer is achieved from the metal clusters to graphene and was enhanced due to the presence of the C defect. The presence of the vacancy deformed the graphene QDs and reduced the interatomic distance between the C atoms and the metal atoms, which in turn, facilitated the charge transfer. The strongest charge transfer was observed in the system Ni13/C23H12. Moreover, for the complexes, the band gap values decreased with the increase of size of the graphene QDs but increased when the C vacancy is present. In addition, the ELF plots demonstrated that the metal clusters transfer charge to the graphene QDs. Our study demonstrates how the electrical conductivity and reactivity of the metal/graphene systems can be controlled by increasing the size of the graphene QDs, by adding defects or by using different metal clusters. High electrical conductivities are necessary to avoid the electron-hole recombination, which in turn, favor the catalytic or photocatalytic processes. According to the results, the highest values of electrical conductivity were obtained for the Cu13 clusters adsorbed on graphene QDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by CONACYT. The authors wish to thank to the supercomputer facilities of Xiuhcoatl and Abacus. FMA thanks to Conacyt for the received PhD scholarship. The financial support from the Proyecto Fondecyt #1130984 is recognized.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physe.2019.02.005.
